MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Dec-01-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND IN THE ALTERNATIVE FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Appearances: Glen Robert Olson of Long & Levit LLP (415.438.4483) for plaintiff and moving party; James M. Emery and Scott Reiber of the Office of the City Attorney (415.554.4628) for defendant and moving party.

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: GRANTED. The court rejects plaintiff's argument that the "Tax Collector" actually imposed the penalties on 9/24/13 when the "auditor" issued the notice of tax audit deficiency. Sec. 6.17-2 expressly refers to the "Tax Collector" imposing a penalty. The 9/24/13 notice of tax deficiency provides that plaintiff can contest the findings of the "auditor" by petitioning for a redetermination. Plaintiff in fact petitioned for a redetermination and after the hearing, the Tax Collector found that plaintiff was negligent and the penalties should be imposed. In other words, the "Tax Collector" imposed the penalties when the petition for redetermination was resolved. There was no imposition based on the auditor's notice of tax deficiency. 6.17-2 provides that the "Tax Collector" must find negligence and that is precisely what happened in this case after the redetermination hearing. It is noteworthy that the first document that the "Tax Collector" signed was the decision on the petition for redetermination. There is no authority that the auditor needed to make findings of negligence in the first instance and such a scheme would be contrary to California law because the burden of proof is on the taxpayer. (See Dicon Fiberoptics, Inc. v. Franchise Tax Bd. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 1227, 1235.) The court further finds that the Tax Collector had a sufficient basis to determine that plaintiff acted without ordinary care. The tax collector's determination is entitled to deference and Mr. Tiret admitted that he did not meaningfully review Proposition Q prior to giving his advice to plaintiff. (Tiret Depo., 34:6-36:11.) (See People ex rel. Strumpfer v. Westoaks Investment #27 (2006) 139 Cal.App.4th 1038, 1050.) Order signed. Judge: Gail Dekreon, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Vicki Gross, CSR 5525 (email: vgross@sftc.org) (302/GD)
